question: Arkansas's seat belt law makes it illegal to:
choices: Ride unbelted in the front seat once you are driving past city-limit signs
Drive or ride in the front seat without wearing a safety belt — and it's a primary offense
Drive without a belt yourself, though front-seat passengers usually just choose for themselves
Ride in the front seat without a belt during the hours after sunset each night
explanation: Police can stop you just for an unbelted driver or front-seat passenger (primary enforcement, since 2009). All children under 15 must be properly restrained wherever they sit.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 9 — Arkansas Seat Belt Law

question: Arkansas requires a child to ride in a child safety seat if the child is:
choices: Under 6 years old AND under 60 pounds
Under 3 years old, no matter the weight
Under 4 feet tall, regardless of age
Under 8 years old OR under 80 pounds
explanation: Children younger than 6 who weigh less than 60 pounds must be secured in a child passenger safety seat. All children under 15 must wear appropriate restraints while the vehicle is moving.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 9 — Child Passenger Protection Act (27-34-104)

question: You are involved in a crash where someone is injured or property damage exceeds $1,000. Arkansas law requires you to:
choices: Leave the scene quickly so that you generally avoid blocking the flow of traffic for other people
Simply call up your own insurance company and usually let them report the whole thing to the police
Stay seated inside of your vehicle and typically decline to move it for the police who respond
Stop, move your vehicle off the roadway if possible, and notify the police immediately
explanation: You must stop, move the vehicle so it doesn't block traffic, and notify police immediately when there's injury, death, or damage over $1,000. You can't leave a crash with injury or death until police have talked with you — and crashes with injury, death, or over $3,000 damage must also be reported to the Office of Driver Services within 30 days.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 80-81 — Traffic Crashes

question: When stopped by a law enforcement officer, Arkansas law requires you to provide:
choices: Generally your full legal name and your current home address, and no more
Typically little else until you can phone your own lawyer
Your driver's license, proof of insurance, and vehicle registration when requested
Your driver's license, but usually just if you happened to be stopped for speeding
explanation: You must provide your driver's license, proof of insurance, and registration when an officer requests them. Tell the officer where the documents are and ask before reaching for them.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by Law Enforcement

question: Under Arkansas's distracted driving laws, a driver UNDER 18:
choices: May freely use a cell phone at absolutely any time at all, as long as the phone connection is fully hands-free
May not use a cell phone at all while driving — not even hands-free — except in an emergency
May talk away on a hand-held phone but is simply never allowed to send or read any text message at all
May use a hand-held cell phone freely, but only while driving on rural roads or other low-traffic roads
explanation: Texting is illegal for ALL drivers (27-51-1501). Under 18, no cell phone use is allowed at all, including hands-free. Ages 18-20 may use hands-free only; 21+ may use a phone but it's never truly safe.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 46 — Distracted Driving Law (27-51-1601)

question: To pass the Arkansas vision test, you must have at least:
choices: 20/70 vision in at least one eye, with or without corrective lenses
20/20 vision in both eyes, corrected or not
20/40 vision in both of your eyes, whether corrected with lenses or not
20/100 vision in one eye, corrected or not
explanation: Arkansas requires at least 20/70 vision in at least one eye, with or without corrective lenses. If you have a 'B' restriction, you must wear your corrective lenses while driving or you can be ticketed.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 71 — Vision

question: After a crash with injury, death, or more than $3,000 damage to one person's property, you must file a report with the Office of Driver Services within:
choices: 48 hours
5 days
six months
30 days
explanation: File the accident report within 30 days (someone may file for you if you're injured). Separately, police must be notified immediately when there's injury, death, or damage over $1,000.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 81 — Keep a Record of Crash Information

question: At a crash scene, you should move an injured person:
choices: Onto the nearest sidewalk right away
Into your own car to drive them to a hospital
Only if they are in immediate danger, such as a burning vehicle
Anytime they ask, whatever their injuries
explanation: Don't move the injured unless danger (like fire) forces it. Check breathing, apply direct pressure to bleeding, keep them warm to prevent shock, and don't give them anything to drink.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 81 — Assisting the Injured

question: Emergency flashers (hazard lights) are for:
choices: Helping you see the road better in heavy rain, fog, or snow
Warning others of an emergency, a broken-down vehicle, or that you're parked on the roadside
Giving you permission to park your vehicle anywhere you want for as long as you need to leave it
Signaling drivers behind you that you follow a funeral procession
explanation: Hazards warn other drivers of an emergency or a stopped/broken-down vehicle. In fog or rain, drive with low-beam headlights instead.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 52 — Emergency Flashers

question: Arkansas law on smoking in vehicles makes it illegal to knowingly smoke tobacco in a car carrying:
choices: A child younger than 18 years
More than 2 passengers
Any non-smoker aboard
A child under 14
explanation: The Protection from Secondhand Smoke for Children Act (§20-27-1903) bans smoking in any motor vehicle in which a child under 14 is a passenger.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 9 — Protection from Secondhand Smoke

question: Each written (knowledge) examination in Arkansas costs:
choices: $10.00 plus tax
explanation: Act 1289 of 2015 requires a $5 fee per written exam, collected with the license cost — another good reason to pass on the next try!
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 1 — Introduction (Act 1289)

question: If you have epilepsy, Arkansas allows you to drive if:
choices: You make sure that a licensed adult is usually riding along with you
You are under a doctor's care and have been seizure-free for one year
You generally restrict your driving to the daylight hours, day to day
No — epilepsy typically keeps you from a license
explanation: Epilepsy under medical control is usually not dangerous for driving: the standard is doctor's care plus one year seizure-free.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 72 — Health (Epilepsy)

question: Does holding your phone to take a call, send a text, look up directions, or scroll social media count as a moving traffic violation in Arkansas?
choices: No — generally just the sending or reading of a text message is counted as one
Yes, but generally just on a divided interstate lane
Yes — if you can't use a hands-free connection while driving, don't use your phone
Yes, but the rule against it typically applies to drivers under 21
explanation: Yes — holding your phone for calls, texts, directions, or social media is a moving violation. In a work zone with workers present, that fine DOUBLES.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 19 — Leave Your Phone Alone

question: Sending or reading a text takes your eyes off the road for about 5 seconds. At 55 mph, that is like driving:
choices: The length of one parked car with both of your eyes closed
About half of a city block while you keep staying fully alert ahead
Roughly 100 feet down the road while glancing up now and then
The length of an entire football field with your eyes closed
explanation: Five seconds at 55 mph covers about a football field — blind. Texting is the most alarming distraction because it combines all three types at once.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 45 — What is Distracted Driving?

question: The three types of distraction involved in texting or hand-held phone use are:
choices: Visual, manual, and cognitive — eyes off the road, hands off the wheel, mind off driving
Audio, video, and social — ears off the road, eyes off the wheel, focus off driving
Legal, financial, and physical — fear of tickets, costs, and fatigue
Daytime, nighttime, and weather — the three conditions that pull focus from driving
explanation: Texting hits all three at once: VISUAL (eyes off the road), MANUAL (hands off the wheel), and COGNITIVE (mind off driving). That's what makes it the most dangerous distraction.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 46 — Dangerous Distractions

question: Compared with other drivers, teens who text while driving are about how much more likely to crash?
choices: About 40 times
23 times
About 60 times
100 times
explanation: National research: distracted teens texting behind the wheel are 23 times more likely to be involved in a crash — and those crashes are often worse because there's no reaction before impact.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 17 — Work Zone Safety (Distracted Driving)

question: You hit a parked car and can't find the owner. Arkansas requires you to:
choices: Drive away from the scene, since the damage done to the parked car is really the other owner's problem now
Leave a written note under the wiper if the damage looks costly
Try to locate the owner, and notify police immediately if anyone is injured or property damage exceeds $1,000
Wait beside the parked car for about five minutes, then go on your way
explanation: In a crash with a parked vehicle you must try to locate the owner. Police must be notified immediately for any injury, death, or damage over $1,000.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 80 — Traffic Crashes

question: You drive past a crash that does not involve you, and emergency help has already arrived. You should:
choices: Pull over to the shoulder, come to a complete stop, and take some photographs of the crash and the vehicles
Park your vehicle somewhere nearby on the side of the road so that you are able to safely watch what happens next
Honk your horn several times as you slowly pass by the crash to show the emergency responders your full support
Keep moving and focus on your driving — stopping or driving to crash scenes blocks the way for rescue vehicles
explanation: Don't stop at a crash unless you're involved or help hasn't arrived. Spectators block police, ambulances, and tow trucks — keep moving and watch for people near the road.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 80 — Traffic Crashes

question: At a crash scene with a wrecked vehicle, you should turn off the ignition and:
choices: Not smoke anywhere near it — fuel may have spilled and could start a fire
You should light a road flare and set it down right beside the wreck
Push the wrecked car off of the road right away, generally by yourself
Usually disconnect both of the vehicle's battery cables before you do anything else
explanation: Turn off wrecked vehicles' ignitions and don't smoke near them — spilled fuel ignites. Stay away from any downed electrical wires, and don't stand in traffic lanes.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 80 — Traffic Crashes

question: Which is the study guide's advice about driving while tired?
choices: Never drive if you are sleepy — it is better to stop and sleep for a few hours than take a chance you can stay awake
A few good cups of strong black coffee right before you go should make a long overnight drive safe to take on
Rolling the car windows the whole way down will generally keep almost any tired driver wide awake at the wheel
Eating a large, heavy meal right before setting out on a long road trip should help to keep yourself alert
explanation: If you're sleepy, stop and sleep. Plan trips for when you're rested, avoid drowsy-making medicines, eat light, take regular breaks — and avoid driving during hours your body expects to rest.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 72 — Fatigue

question: On a 90-degree day, about how quickly does the inside of a parked car reach the outdoor temperature — and then keep climbing?
choices: Only after a full hour does the inside air finally begin to warm up
The air conditioning keeps the whole cabin cool for about 30 minutes
A parked car will not heat up at all as long as the windows are cracked
Within 5 minutes, rising about 15 degrees every 9 minutes after
explanation: Within five minutes the interior matches the outside temperature, then gains ~15° every 9 minutes. Never leave children or pets unattended in a vehicle.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 10 — Unattended Children and Pets

question: Which statement about deaf or hearing-impaired drivers is TRUE?
choices: By law they are generally permitted to drive just during the daytime hours and not once it has gotten dark out at night
By law they typically cannot drive on an interstate highway, and should use just city streets and back roads instead
Studies show their driving records are as good as drivers with normal hearing — they adjust by relying on vision
By law they should generally carry along a licensed passenger who is able to hear clearly out on the road with them
explanation: Hearing helps (horns, sirens), but deaf and hearing-impaired drivers compensate by staying visually alert — and their records are as good as anyone's.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 71 — Hearing

question: Arkansas requires a driver's license or certificate to operate which of the following on public highways?
choices: Generally just passenger cars and pickup trucks, nothing else
A car, motorcycle, motor-driven cycle, or motorized bicycle
Typically a vehicle whose motor is larger than 50 cubic centimeters
Cars and trucks, but usually not motorcycles or mopeds
explanation: Anyone who operates a motor vehicle, motorcycle, motor-driven cycle, or motorized bicycle on public highways must have a license or certificate.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 2 — The Driver's License

question: If you move to Arkansas and become a resident, how soon must you obtain an Arkansas driver's license?
choices: Within 30 days
Within about 10 days
Within 60 days
Within 90 days
explanation: A new resident must obtain an Arkansas license within 30 days. A nonresident who has been physically present in the state for 6 months must also get one (27-16-606).
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 2 — The Driver's License (27-16-606)

question: The Class D license — Arkansas's regular non-restricted driver's license — is issued to persons who are at least:
choices: age 14
age 16
at least 21 years old
18 years old
explanation: A Class D (non-restricted) license is for those 18 or older who have held an instruction permit and passed the knowledge, vision, and skills tests.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 4 — Class D License

question: Under Arkansas's graduated-license rules, sending text messages while driving is:
choices: Allowed once a driver turns 21 years of age
Allowed if it is done with a hands-free device
Not allowed regardless of the driver's age
Allowed on local city streets but not highways
explanation: Text messaging is not allowed regardless of age. A Class D driver 18-21 may use a hands-free device; a hand-held phone is for emergencies only; under 18, no phone use is allowed at all.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 4 — Class D License

question: To ride a motorcycle on Arkansas roadways, a driver needs a:
choices: Class D license, nothing more
Class M (Motorcycle) license
Motorized Bicycle Certificate
Commercial Driver License
explanation: A Class M license authorizes motorcycle operation (age 16+). It requires a motorcycle knowledge exam, a vision test, and a motorcycle skills exam — the skills exam can be waived by completing a Motorcycle Safety Foundation Riders Course.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 4 — Class M (Motorcycle) License

question: The Class MD (Motor-Driven Cycle) license, for ages 14-16, restricts the rider to motorcycles displacing:
choices: 250 cc or less
50 cc or less
150 cubic centimeters or less
Any size at all
explanation: A Class MD license is restricted to motorcycles displacing 250 cubic centimeters or less. It requires two knowledge exams, a vision test, and a motorcycle skills exam.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 4 — Class MD (Motor Driven Cycles)

question: A Motorized Bicycle Certificate (ages 14-16) allows operation of a motorized bicycle or motorcycle displacing no more than:
choices: 5 cc
8 cc
2 cc
50 cc
explanation: The Motorized Bicycle Certificate restricts operation to 50 cc or less. Anyone already holding a Class A, B, C, D, Intermediate, Learner's, M, or MD license may also operate a motorized bicycle of 50 cc or less.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 5 — Motorized Bicycle (MB) Certificate

question: A driver with an Intermediate License generally may NOT drive between which hours unless a licensed driver 21+ is along (or they are going to/from school, church, a job, or an emergency)?
choices: 9:00 pm and 5:00 am
11:00 pm and 4:00 am
midnight to 6 am
10 pm and 5:00 am
explanation: Intermediate License holders cannot drive between 11:00 pm and 4:00 am unless accompanied by a licensed driver 21 or older, or driving to/from a school activity, church activity, job, or an emergency.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 4 — Intermediate License

question: An Intermediate License holder driving without a licensed adult in the front seat may carry:
choices: Generally as many passengers as they might want to bring
Usually just passengers who are family members
No more than one unrelated minor passenger
Typically no passengers riding in the vehicle with them
explanation: Without a licensed driver 21+ in the front seat, an Intermediate driver may not carry more than one unrelated minor passenger. All passengers must wear seat belts.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 4 — Intermediate License

question: A vehicle designed to carry 16 or more passengers, haul hazardous material, or with a gross weight rating of 26,001 pounds or more requires:
choices: A regular Class D license
A learner's permit
A motorcycle endorsement
A Commercial License
explanation: Instruction permits, Learner's, Intermediate, and Class D licenses cover four-wheel vehicles under 26,001 lbs GVWR. Bigger vehicles, 16+ passenger vehicles, or hazmat haulers require a Commercial Driver License.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 3 — Types of Graduated Driver's Licenses

question: An UNRESTRICTED Arkansas license requires 20/40 uncorrected vision. The minimum CORRECTED vision for a RESTRICTED license (must wear glasses or contacts) is:
explanation: 20/40 uncorrected earns an unrestricted license; 20/70 corrected earns a restricted license that requires wearing glasses or contacts while driving.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 5 — Vision Requirements (27-16-704)

question: Arkansas's vision standard requires a field of vision of at least:
choices: 90 degrees for a person with two functional eyes, or 60 degrees with just one eye
140 degrees for a person with two functional eyes (105 degrees with one eye)
180 degrees for a licensed driver, generally with very few exceptions allowed
Arkansas generally sets no field-of-vision requirement of its own for driving
explanation: Field of vision must be 140 degrees for a person with two functional eyes, or 105 degrees for a person with one functional eye. A vision test is valid for one year.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 5 — Vision Requirements

question: Your license shows a 'B' restriction. While driving, you:
choices: May leave your glasses at home if the trip is a short one
Generally need to put on your corrective lenses after dark
Must wear your corrective lenses, or you can be ticketed
Usually need your corrective lenses just out on the highway
explanation: A 'B' restriction means you must drive with your corrective lenses. If stopped by law enforcement, you can be ticketed for failing to wear them. Keep an extra pair in the vehicle.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 71 — Vision

question: The study guide warns that wearing dark or tinted glasses or contact lenses at night:
choices: Is the single best way there is to cut down on oncoming headlight glare
Is actually required by Arkansas law any time you are driving after dark
Will noticeably sharpen and improve your night vision out on the road
Cuts down too much light — and you need light to see the road ahead
explanation: Even though tinted lenses reduce glare, they cut out too much light at night, when you need all the light you can get to see the road. If glare bothers you at night, slow down and be careful.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 71 — Vision (Night Vision)

question: Because you cannot focus clearly on things to the side while looking ahead, the manual says to take advantage of your peripheral vision by:
choices: Using your side mirrors and glancing to the side
Fixing your stare on the center line ahead
Wearing wraparound sunglasses
Turning your whole body around to look aside
explanation: Peripheral vision — the field of vision from the corner of your eye — helps you spot trouble on either side. Use your side mirrors and glance to the side, since you can't focus on things to the side while looking ahead.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 71 — Vision (Peripheral Vision)

question: The study guide advises that a driver with diabetes who takes insulin should NOT drive when:
choices: It happens to be raining hard enough outside to noticeably reduce how far down the road they are able to see
There is a chance of going into shock — for example after skipping a meal or taking the wrong amount of insulin
It has gotten dark out for the night, because taking insulin sharply lowers a person's ability to see in the dark
They have just finished eating a full meal, since digestion makes insulin users drowsy
explanation: Diabetics on insulin shouldn't drive when there's a risk of going into shock (from a skipped meal/snack or the wrong insulin dose). Have someone else drive when adjusting insulin, and get your eyes checked for night blindness.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 72 — Health (Diabetes)

question: A person with heart disease, high blood pressure, or circulation problems:
choices: Is honestly not affected in a single way while they are out behind the wheel of a car
May generally be restricted by law to driving cars that have an automatic transmission
May risk blackouts, fainting, or a heart attack and should ask a doctor before driving
Should generally drive at just those times when another licensed adult is in the car
explanation: These conditions can bring on blackouts, fainting, or a heart attack. If you're being treated for a heart condition, ask your doctor before driving.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 72 — Health (Heart Condition)

question: If you are very angry or excited, the study guide recommends you:
choices: Drive a bit faster than usual to blow off some steam
Turn the music up loud to take your mind off whatever is bothering you
Give yourself time to calm down — take a short walk and stay off the road until you have cooled off
Stick to the wide-open interstate, where the steady pace settles you down
explanation: Strong emotions affect driving ability. When angry or excited, calm down first — take a short walk and stay off the road until you've cooled off. If you're impatient, leave a few minutes early.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 72 — Health (Emotional Distress)

question: According to the manual, which of these can affect your ability to drive safely?
choices: Generally just a genuinely serious illness affects driving
Even a bad cold, a stiff neck, a cough, or a sore leg
Typically little short of a real disability affects driving
Usually just a doctor-diagnosed condition affects driving
explanation: Many health problems — even a bad cold, infection, stiff neck, cough, or sore leg — can affect your driving. If you aren't feeling well and have places to go, let someone else drive.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 72 — Health

question: To avoid getting drowsy on a long trip, the study guide suggests you:
choices: Eat light meals, avoid drowsy-making medicine, and take regular breaks
Eat one large, full meal right before you leave so you won't get hungry
Drink several cups of strong black coffee and just push on through the trip
Drive the whole way with every one of the windows rolled completely down
explanation: Large meals tend to cause drowsiness, so eat light; don't take medicine that makes you drowsy; and stop regularly to walk around and refresh. If you're sleepy, stop and sleep.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 72 — Fatigue

question: In Arkansas, a first littering conviction can bring a fine of up to:
choices: $100 and up to 2 hours of community service
$500 and up to 4 hours of community service
$5,000 and up to 40 hours of community service
$1,000 and up to 8 hours of community service
explanation: Littering is against the law. A first offense brings a fine up to $1,000 and up to 8 hours of community service; later convictions can reach $2,000 and 24 hours of community service.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 10 — Arkansas Law on Littering

question: To legally park in an accessible (handicapped) parking space, you must have:
choices: Any vehicle whatsoever, just so long as you are only parked there briefly for a quick errand
A clearly handwritten note left out in plain view across the top of your vehicle's dashboard
The proper license plate with the international symbol, or a Special Certificate hang tag
A business card belonging to your personal doctor displayed somewhere in the front windshield
explanation: You need either the license plate bearing the international symbol or the designated hang tag ('Special Certificate'). The certificate holder must be in the vehicle, and the tag hangs from the rearview mirror or sits on the dashboard.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 14 — AR Governor's Commission on People with Disabilities

question: Using another person's disabled-parking Special Certificate is:
choices: Generally a minor infraction that carries just a small fine
Fraud — a Class A misdemeanor with fines and a possible license suspension
Usually fine if you happen to be on your way to pick that person up
Typically a problem just if you actually end up getting a ticket
explanation: Illegally using someone else's Special Certificate is fraud (a Class A misdemeanor). A first offense is $100-$500; a second is $250-$1,000 plus a 6-month license suspension.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 14 — AR Governor's Commission on People with Disabilities

question: After you pass your driver tests in Arkansas, you are asked whether you wish to be an organ and tissue donor. If you say yes:
choices: The words 'Organ Donor' are printed on the front of your license and you are listed in a state registry
Generally nothing about your choice is recorded by the state, and it stays between you and the clerk
You are typically handed a separate paper donor card that you are then expected to carry around with you
Your decision is usually treated as permanent and can't afterward be changed
explanation: Applicants are asked at testing. Those who choose to participate have 'Organ Donor' printed on the license and are listed in a state registry that helps medical personnel identify donors.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 76 — Arkansas Organ & Tissue Donor Information

question: Which statement about organ and tissue donation is TRUE according to the study guide?
choices: Choosing to register as an organ donor lowers the quality of the emergency medical care that you will receive
The donor's own surviving family is billed in full for every one of the costs of the organ-harvesting process afterward
Donating your organs noticeably changes the way that the body looks and appears later on at an open-casket funeral
The decision will NOT influence the quality of your emergency care, and your family pays nothing for the donation
explanation: Donation only occurs after every effort to save your life. Being a donor does not affect the quality of your care, donors are treated with respect and dignity, and the donor's family is not charged for the donation.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 76 — Arkansas Organ & Tissue Donor Information

question: When you are stopped by a law enforcement officer, you should:
choices: Step out of your vehicle right away as soon as you stop so that you can meet the officer about halfway
Have all of your documents already gathered in your hand before the officer reaches your side window
Keep both hands visible — for example at the top of the steering wheel — and avoid sudden movements
Keep your foot resting on the gas pedal so that the engine stays revved up while you wait there for them
explanation: Stay in the vehicle unless told to exit, keep both hands visible (such as at the top of the steering wheel), and don't make sudden movements while looking for your documents.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by a Law Enforcement Officer

question: If you are pulled over at night, the study guide says you should:
choices: You should step out of the car so that the officer is able to get a clear look at you
Turn on your interior (dome) lights so the officer can see you more clearly
You might flash your high-beam headlights toward the officer as they walk up to you
Generally switch off the lights on and inside your vehicle until the officer arrives
explanation: At night, turn on the interior lights so the officer can see inside the vehicle clearly. Also turn off the radio, put down any devices, and give the officer your full attention.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by a Law Enforcement Officer

question: If there is a weapon in your vehicle when you are stopped by an officer, you should:
choices: Pick up the weapon and hand it directly over to the officer right away
Quietly slide the weapon down out of sight underneath one of the vehicle's seats
Say nothing at all to the officer about the weapon unless they happen to ask about it
Inform the officer of the weapon and its location — and do NOT reach for it
explanation: Tell the officer there is a weapon, what type it is, and where it is — but do not reach for it. Always follow the officer's instructions regarding any weapon in the vehicle.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by a Law Enforcement Officer

question: Signing a traffic citation:
choices: Is a promise to appear in court or pay the fine — NOT an admission of guilt; refusing to sign can lead to arrest
Generally counts as a binding admission that you are in fact guilty of the violation cited
Usually means you have given up your right to take it before a judge later
Is typically an optional formality that carries no real consequences
explanation: Signing a citation is not an admission of guilt — it's a promise to appear in court or pay the fine before the court date. Refusing to sign, if required, can result in a physical arrest.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by a Law Enforcement Officer

question: If you are unsure whether the person trying to stop you is an actual police officer, you should:
choices: Hit the gas pedal hard and just speed away as fast as you possibly can from the vehicle behind you
Activate your turn signal, drive to the nearest well-lit location, and dial 9-1-1 to confirm
Pull over and come to a stop right away on the shoulder of the very next bridge or overpass you reach
Pay them no attention whatsoever and simply keep on driving along the road exactly as you normally would
explanation: If you're unsure, activate your turn signal, pull to the nearest well-lit location, dial 9-1-1, and request confirmation that it is a real officer.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by a Law Enforcement Officer

question: When pulling over for an officer, you should stop:
choices: On the very next bridge or overpass that you happen to come to along the way
Right where you are at, in the middle of the travel lane you are driving along in
At the closest safe spot out of the traffic lane — not on bridges or overpasses
Wherever you happen to be at that exact moment, stopping there
explanation: Pull to the right and stop at the closest safe spot out of the traffic lane. Do not stop on bridges or overpasses.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by a Law Enforcement Officer

question: If you wish to contest a citation, you should:
choices: Make your full case and argue the whole thing out with the officer right there at the roadside
Refuse to accept or take the paper ticket from the officer's hand so that it can never become official
Insist firmly that a supervising officer be sent out right away to the scene of the stop to hear you out
Contact the court listed on the citation — do not argue with the officer on the side of the road
explanation: Be courteous even if you disagree. To contest a citation, contact the court listed on it; don't argue with the officer at the roadside.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by a Law Enforcement Officer

question: An applicant under 18 applying for an Arkansas license or permit must provide a U.S. birth certificate and:
choices: The signature of a parent or legal guardian (or a completed ASP-33 affidavit)
Nothing further at all beyond the birth certificate itself is required
A diploma or transcript showing that they have already graduated from a high school
Copies of two full years' worth of signed federal tax returns
explanation: Under-18 applicants need an original/certified U.S. birth certificate, a parent or legal guardian's signature (or an ASP-33 Financial Responsibility affidavit), and one accepted secondary ID.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 7 — Accepted Identification (Under Age 18)

question: If you have a concealed carry permit and are stopped by an officer, you should:
choices: Generally keep the permit to yourself and not bring it up at the stop
Present it to the officer along with your driver's license
Mention the permit usually just if the officer asks you about it
You should step out of the vehicle and discuss the permit in person
explanation: Present your concealed carry permit to the officer with your driver's license. Also tell the officer where any weapon is located, and do not reach for it.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide — What To Do When Stopped by a Law Enforcement Officer

question: Failing to move over or slow down for a stopped emergency or law-enforcement vehicle with its lights on (the Move Over Law) can bring:
choices: Generally just a simple spoken warning from the officer the first time
A flat $20 ticket and generally no other penalty beyond paying that one small fine
A fine, jail time, community service, and a license suspension of up to 6 months
Typically no penalty the very first time that you are caught
explanation: On a multilane highway you must move to the farthest lane from a stopped emergency/law-enforcement vehicle, or slow down if changing lanes isn't possible. Violations can mean fines, jail, community service, and up to a 6-month license suspension.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 11 — Move Over Law

question: Emergency and authorized vehicles in Arkansas may display which light colors?
choices: Generally just purple lights, no others
Usually blue, with no other color being permitted
Typically green, with no other color permitted
Yellow, red, red & white, red & blue, or blue
explanation: Emergency vehicles display yellow, red, red & white, red & blue, or blue lights. When one approaches with lights and siren, pull to the right edge and stop; move over or slow down for stopped ones.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 15 — Approaching a Law Enforcement Vehicle with Emergency Lights On

question: Which testing resource is officially offered by the Arkansas State Police?
choices: An app called 'AR Driver Testing' in the Google Play or Apple app store
Usually one of the many third-party driving practice-test websites
A paid one-on-one tutoring service generally run from the police office
Generally there is no official testing resource that is available
explanation: The Arkansas State Police is NOT associated with third-party practice-test websites. It does offer an official testing app, 'AR Driver Testing,' in the Google Play and Apple app stores.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 1 — Introduction

question: When you go to take your driver tests, the study guide reminds you that if you wear glasses or contact lenses, you should:
choices: Bring them with you to the test
Leave them at home for the eye test
Bring them only to drive
Buy a brand-new pair before testing
explanation: If you wear contacts or glasses, bring them with you to test — you'll need them for the vision test and, if you require corrective lenses, your license will carry a restriction to wear them while driving.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 7 — Accepted Identification

question: The motorcycle SKILLS exam for a Class M license can be waived if the applicant:
choices: Is already over the age of 21 at the time that they go in to apply for the new motorcycle license at the office
Already holds a valid and unexpired Arkansas Class D passenger-car driver's license at the time they apply
Takes and successfully passes the written motorcycle knowledge test on two completely separate occasions
Completes a Motorcycle Safety Foundation Riders Course (and returns the certificate within 90 days)
explanation: The motorcycle skills exam can be waived by successfully completing a Motorcycle Safety Foundation Riders Course; the certificate must be returned to an Arkansas State Police examiner within 90 days of finishing the course.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 4 — Class M (Motorcycle) License

question: Drivers from certain countries with a reciprocal agreement may be issued an Arkansas license without testing. Which is on that exempt list?
choices: Argentina
Indonesia
Germany
Australia
explanation: Countries exempt from testing (by reciprocal agreement) include Manitoba (Canada), Germany, France, Taiwan, and South Korea. Those applicants are referred to the Department of Finance and Administration.
citation: AR Driver License Study Guide, p. 5 — Countries Exempt from Testing
